steps.title: Neurology
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Tertiary Care

steps.title: Acute Weakness
steps.text: What is the priority in managing a child with suspected GBS or polio who shows signs of respiratory compromise?
steps.rationale: The guideline advises airway assessment and respiratory support, including possible intubation, for suspected GBS or polio with airway compromise. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Assess airway and prepare for possible intubation and respiratory support
Start corticosteroids immediately and monitor breathing every 6 hours
Administer bronchodilators and re-evaluate after 12 hours
Provide supportive care and arrange outpatient follow-up

steps.title: Acute Weakness
steps.text: What is the appropriate tertiary-level management approach for a spinal tumor causing neurological symptoms?
steps.rationale: Treatment may include surgery and/or chemotherapy, and palliative care should be considered if curative treatment is not feasible. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Start empiric TB treatment and observe
Delay intervention until biopsy confirms diagnosis
Begin steroids and discharge for outpatient oncology review
Consider surgery or chemotherapy, and offer palliative care if needed

steps.title: Acute Weakness
steps.text: How should tertiary facilities manage a child presenting with acute weakness due to organophosphate poisoning?
steps.rationale: The guideline directs providers to follow the standard protocol for organophosphate poisoning, which typically includes atropine and supportive care. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Refer to secondary care for decontamination
Manage according to the poisoning protocol
Begin high-dose antibiotics
Observe closely without intervention unless symptoms worsen

steps.title: Acute Weakness
steps.text: What is the recommended intervention for thrombotic disease presenting with acute neurological signs?
steps.rationale: For thrombotic disease, the guideline recommends prophylactic aspirin as the primary treatment at the tertiary level. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Begin full anticoagulation immediately
Refer for surgical thrombectomy
Start prophylactic aspirin
Perform lumbar puncture to confirm diagnosis

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: Which of the following is essential in the acute management of a stroke patient?
steps.rationale: Monitoring fluid balance is vital to avoid both dehydration and fluid overload, especially in neurocritical care. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Maintain hyperglycaemia to preserve brain function
Limit monitoring to twice daily assessments
Avoid fluid administration entirely
Monitor fluid input and output carefully

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: In haemorrhagic stroke, what is the appropriate approach to managing elevated blood pressure?
steps.rationale: Antihypertensives must be used carefully to avoid hypoperfusion and maintain cerebral blood flow. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Aggressively lower BP with high-dose diuretics
Ignore BP unless it exceeds 200 mmHg
Stop all medications that affect BP
Use antihypertensives cautiously with frequent BP monitoring

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: What is one of the earliest signs of raised intracranial pressure in a conscious child?
steps.rationale: Vomiting and headache, particularly if worsening or occurring with position changes, can indicate rising ICP. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Frequent urination
Vomiting and worsening headache
Dry skin
Decreased appetite

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: Which of the following interventions helps reduce raised intracranial pressure (ICP)?
steps.rationale: Elevating the head improves venous return and lowers ICP—an essential neuroprotective step. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Encouraging supine positioning
Starting antibiotics
Increasing oral fluid intake
Elevating the head of the bed to at least 30 degrees

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: In ischaemic stroke in children, what determines the decision to start aspirin?
steps.rationale: Aspirin may be used in ischaemic stroke only after senior consultation and assessment for bleeding risk. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: MRI scan results
Discussion with consultant and absence of haemorrhagic risk
Age of the child
Presence of fever

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: What is an appropriate component of supportive stroke care in a high-dependency setting?
steps.rationale: Hourly turning reduces risk of pressure injuries, an important part of stroke nursing care. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Encourage complete bed rest for days
Avoid turning to prevent dislodging IV lines
Limit nutrition until full recovery
Turn hourly to prevent pressure sores

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: What is recommended as part of long-term stroke management in children?
steps.rationale: Early and continued rehabilitation, including physiotherapy, is critical for maximizing recovery. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Discontinue therapy once the child is stable
Continue physiotherapy tailored to neurological severity
Avoid involving caregivers to reduce burden
Restrict activity to prevent further strokes

steps.title: Hydrocephalus
steps.text: What is an important consideration when treating hydrocephalus with ventriculitis at secondary or tertiary level?
steps.rationale: The guideline notes that ventriculitis may require prolonged parenteral therapy, which can extend beyond typical meningitis treatment durations. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: It may require longer parenteral treatment than typical meningitis
Oral antibiotics are sufficient in most cases
Treat only after surgical intervention
Avoid antibiotics until CSF is drained

steps.title: Hydrocephalus
steps.text: What is the recommended emergency intervention for a child with acute raised intracranial pressure (ICP) due to hydrocephalus?
steps.rationale: A therapeutic ventricular tap is advised to relieve acute raised ICP while awaiting definitive treatment. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Administer high-dose steroids and observe
Order urgent CT and delay intervention
Perform a therapeutic ventricular tap
Refer only if symptoms persist after 48 hours

steps.title: Hydrocephalus
steps.text: While awaiting surgery in a child with communicating hydrocephalus, what medical treatment can help reduce CSF production?
steps.rationale: Acetazolamide or Furosemide can be used to temporarily reduce CSF production in communicating hydrocephalus. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Paracetamol and bed rest
Mannitol infusion
Broad-spectrum antibiotics
Acetazolamide or Furosemide

steps.title: Hydrocephalus
steps.text: What is the definitive treatment option for hydrocephalus at tertiary level?
steps.rationale: The guideline recommends ventriculo-peritoneal (VP) shunt or endoscopic third ventriculostomy (ETV) as the definitive surgical treatments for hydrocephalus. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Repeat doses of diuretics
Neurosurgical referral for VP shunt or ETV
Monitoring with supportive care only
Intermittent ventricular taps

steps.title: Blocked Shunts
steps.text: What is the first step in managing a child with a blocked VP shunt and suspected complications at the tertiary level?
steps.rationale: Tertiary-level care should include ABCCCD management and IV Ceftriaxone, mirroring meningitis protocol, as initial steps. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Initiate ABCCCD and start IV antibiotics as for meningitis
Wait for neurosurgical consult before initiating treatment
Begin anticonvulsants immediately regardless of symptoms
Refer the child to outpatient rehabilitation

steps.title: Blocked Shunts
steps.text: When is referral to neurosurgery indicated in a child with a blocked or infected VP shunt?
steps.rationale: The guideline advises neurosurgical referral for possible shunt removal and later revision/replacement if the shunt is blocked or infected. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Only if antibiotics fail after 72 hours
If imaging shows no CSF flow
When the shunt is blocked or infected
Only if neurological signs are worsening

steps.title: Blocked Shunts
steps.text: Why is it important to start IV Ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg daily in children with suspected blocked shunt infections?
steps.rationale: Blocked shunts are treated similarly to meningitis; IV Ceftriaxone is started empirically before surgical intervention. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: It helps reduce intracranial pressure
It is part of the empiric treatment protocol for suspected meningitis-related infections
It prevents seizures associated with hydrocephalus
It replaces the need for neurosurgical intervention

steps.title: Blocked Shunts
steps.text: Which of the following best describes the comprehensive tertiary-level approach to managing a child with a blocked VP shunt and hydrocephalus?
steps.rationale: The guideline emphasizes a multidisciplinary approach involving medical and surgical management, along with physiotherapy, occupational therapy, nutritional support, sensory assessments, and family support systems. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Refer to a neurosurgeon and initiate home-based care immediately
Focus on surgical correction and nutritional recovery
Prioritize medication, then refer to support groups if needed
Combine medical, surgical, rehabilitative, and psychosocial care

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: What is the role of Baclofen in managing children with cerebral palsy at the tertiary level?
steps.rationale: Baclofen is prescribed to manage spasticity and dystonia, but must be used carefully due to side effects like sedation, hypotonia, and risk of seizures if abruptly stopped. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: It is used to control seizures resistant to standard therapy
It reduces spasticity and dystonia but has significant side effects
It promotes growth and muscle development
It is used to treat feeding difficulties

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: Which of the following is a potential consequence of suddenly stopping Baclofen?
steps.rationale: The guideline highlights that abrupt discontinuation of Baclofen may lead to seizures, making proper tapering essential. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Hypertonia and tremors
Cardiac arrhythmia
Seizures
Elevated blood pressure

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: What is the correct use of Diazepam in tertiary care for a child with cerebral palsy?
steps.rationale: Diazepam is used at tertiary level to reduce spasticity, with initial doses between 1–2.5 mg orally every 4–6 hours, adjusted per response. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Antispasmodic treatment at 1–2.5 mg orally every 4–6 hours, adjusted as needed
Used only as a rescue drug during epileptic seizures
Sedative for children with anxiety and behavioral challenges
Given intravenously during physiotherapy

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: In addition to spasticity treatment, what other medical intervention is emphasized for tertiary care in children with CP?
steps.rationale: Antiepileptic treatment is a key part of tertiary care, with medication choices guided by standardized seizure protocols. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Growth hormone therapy
Inhaled corticosteroids
Antiepileptic drugs following seizure protocols
Routine antibiotics for contracture pain

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: What is the orthopedic focus in tertiary management of cerebral palsy?
steps.rationale: Orthopedic care in tertiary settings includes managing contractures, fractures, and acute hip problems, often requiring surgical input. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Addressing contractures, fractures, and acute hip disorders
Providing custom footwear and splints only
Manual muscle strengthening without surgical input
Avoiding surgery unless the child can walk

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: What is the role of education planning in tertiary-level CP care?
steps.rationale: Tertiary care involves assessing each child’s level of function and coordinating appropriate educational placement or support. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Refer all children to special schools
Explore education options based on the child’s functional level
Wait for school-age before initiating plans
Focus only on vocational training

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: What combination of services represents the comprehensive rehabilitation approach in tertiary care for CP?
steps.rationale: A multidisciplinary rehabilitation approach includes PT, ST, audiology, and vision services to improve quality of life and function. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Physiotherapy, speech therapy, audiology, and visual assessment
Antispasmodics and home-based care only
Special education referral and routine follow-up
Antibiotics and general pediatric monitoring

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: What is a correct statement about Baclofen use for spasticity and dystonia in children with cerebral palsy?
steps.rationale: Baclofen is used for spasticity and dystonia in CP, at a dose of 1–2 mg/kg every 8 hours. It may cause sedation, hypotonia, and other CNS-related side effects, and must be used with careful monitoring. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: It is primarily used for seizure prevention in patients with concurrent epilepsy
It is dosed at 1–2 mg/kg every 8 hours and may cause sedation or hypotonia
It is a short-acting muscle relaxant typically reserved for emergency use
It is preferred over physical therapy in mild cases due to fewer side effects

steps.title: Epilepsy
steps.text: What is the recommended approach when seizure control is not achieved on a single antiepileptic drug at therapeutic dose?
steps.rationale: If monotherapy fails, the next step is to add another drug and titrate both carefully while monitoring for efficacy and side effects. [cite: 2]

textChoices.text: Increase the dose beyond the therapeutic range
Stop the current drug immediately and start a new one
Add a second drug and optimize both gradually
Use only non-pharmacological treatments

steps.title: Epilepsy
steps.text: Which of the following is a key principle of rational combination therapy in epilepsy management?
steps.rationale: Rational combination therapy aims to improve efficacy and minimize interactions by combining medications with different mechanisms of action. [cite: 2]

textChoices.text: Use drugs from the same class to enhance potency
Combine drugs with different mechanisms of action
Always use three drugs simultaneously
Avoid second-line medications

steps.title: Epilepsy
steps.text: Why is sodium valproate generally avoided in females of childbearing potential?
steps.rationale: Sodium valproate is associated with teratogenic risks and adverse neurodevelopmental outcomes in the unborn child. [cite: 2]

textChoices.text: It is ineffective in generalised epilepsy
It causes significant weight loss
It carries a risk of teratogenicity and neurodevelopmental impairment
It requires intravenous administration

steps.title: Epilepsy
steps.text: Withdrawal of antiepileptic medication should be considered only after:
steps.rationale: Medication withdrawal is only advised after a minimum of 2 years without seizures and should be gradual and well-supervised. [cite: 2]

textChoices.text: 6 months of seizure freedom
Switching to another class of drug
At least 2 years of being seizure-free
The patient starts school

steps.title: Epilepsy
steps.text: Which of the following must be ruled out when managing a patient with “Intractable Epilepsy”?
steps.rationale: One key reason for poor seizure control is misclassification of the epilepsy type, which can lead to inappropriate treatment. [cite: 2]

textChoices.text: Poor socioeconomic background
Incorrect classification of epilepsy syndrome
Simple febrile seizures
Migraine

steps.title: Epilepsy
steps.text: What is a possible indication for epilepsy surgery in a patient with refractory seizures?
steps.rationale: Patients with focal seizures due to identifiable brain lesions may benefit from surgical evaluation. [cite: 2]

textChoices.text: Polytherapy is effective
Lesional epilepsy identified on imaging
Seizures are improving over time
Patient has no structural brain abnormalities

steps.title: Neural Tube Defects
steps.text: What is a key feature of tertiary-level care in managing neural tube defects?
steps.rationale: Tertiary-level care involves coordinated input from multiple specialties, often led by surgery, to address the complex needs of the child. [cite: 2]

textChoices.text: Independent management by a single specialist
Exclusive reliance on outpatient follow-up
A multidisciplinary team approach led by the surgical team
Discharge to home care after surgical closure

steps.title: Neural Tube Defects
steps.text: When is it appropriate to step down care from tertiary to district hospital for a child with a neural tube defect?
steps.rationale: Care can be transferred to a district hospital once treatment has been properly initiated and complications are under control. [cite: 2]

textChoices.text: Once the baby is stabilized and gains weight
After the initial surgical consult is completed
Once treatment is established and complications are managed
Only after completion of physiotherapy

steps.title: Headache
steps.text: Which of the following is a recommended strategy when managing recurrent headaches in children?
steps.rationale: Keeping a detailed diary helps identify headache patterns and triggers, which is a foundational step in headache management. [cite: 2]

textChoices.text: Start medication immediately without diary tracking
Keep a diary recording quality, severity, timing, and triggers
Advise complete school absence until headache-free
Avoid all physical activity for at least one week

steps.title: Headache
steps.text: What advice should be given regarding school attendance for children with recurrent headaches?
steps.rationale: Children should return to school, and short breaks (e.g., 15 minutes in the nurse’s office during peak headache) are acceptable to help them cope. [cite: 2]

textChoices.text: Keep them home until headaches fully resolve
Encourage return to school with planned breaks if needed
Restrict school participation to half-days permanently
Recommend homeschooling due to stress factors

steps.title: Headache
steps.text: Which of the following is not a recommended lifestyle modification for children with recurrent headaches?
steps.rationale: Overuse of analgesics can worsen headaches; medication overuse is a known risk factor for chronic headaches. [cite: 2]

textChoices.text: Avoiding headache triggers like caffeine and poor sleep
Daily physical exercise
Using painkillers daily to prevent headache onset
Managing comorbid sleep problems

steps.title: Headache
steps.text: What is an important goal of patient education in headache management?
steps.rationale: Empowering patients enhances self-control and can significantly reduce distress and improve outcomes in chronic headache management. [cite: 2]

textChoices.text: Minimize the use of diaries and self-monitoring
Focus solely on pharmacological solutions
Enable patients to manage their disease and reduce psychological distress
Encourage complete avoidance of social interaction
